topics_flat: Algebra > Prealgebra / Basic Algebra > Simple Equations
prompt:
Determine all quadruples $(a, b, c, d)$ of real numbers satisfying the following system of equations.
$$
\begin{aligned}
 ab + ac &= 3b + 3c \\
 bc + bd &= 5c + 5d \\
 ac + cd &= 7a + 7d \\
 ad + bd &= 9a + 9b
\end{aligned}
$$
response_text:
Rewrite each equation by factoring:
\[
\begin{aligned}
ab+ac&=a(b+c)=3(b+c),\\
bc+bd&=b(c+d)=5(c+d),\\
ac+cd&=c(a+d)=7(a+d),\\
ad+bd&=d(a+b)=9(a+b).
\end{aligned}
\]
So the system is equivalent to
\[
(a-3)(b+c)=0,\quad (b-5)(c+d)=0,\quad (c-7)(a+d)=0,\quad (d-9)(a+b)=0.
\]

We now solve by cases.

A useful observation: if none of \(a,b,c,d\) equals \(3,5,7,9\) respectively, then we must have
\[
b+c=0,\quad c+d=0,\quad a+d=0,\quad a+b=0.
\]
From these,
\[
b=-c,\ d=-c,\ a=c,\ a=-b=c,
\]
so \(a=c\) and \(b=d=-a\). Then \(c+d=a-a=0\), forcing \(b=5\) or \(a=0\); similarly from the other equations one gets inconsistency unless \(a=0\), giving
\[
(a,b,c,d)=(0,0,0,0).
\]
Indeed \((0,0,0,0)\) works.

Now consider cases where some variable equals its special value.

Because each equation is of the form \((x-\text{const})(\text{sum})=0\), one easy family comes from making all the sums zero:
\[
b+c=0,\quad c+d=0,\quad a+d=0,\quad a+b=0.
\]
This gives \(a=c,\ b=d=-a\), i.e.
\[
(a,b,c,d)=(t,-t,t,-t).
\]
Substitute into the second equation:
\[
bc+bd=t(-t)+(-t)(-t)=0,\qquad 5(c+d)=0,
\]
so it always works only when the factored form is satisfied. Checking the first gives
\[
(a-3)(b+c)=0
\]
automatically, and similarly all four, so actually every \((t,-t,t,-t)\) with all sums zero must also satisfy all original equations. But \(c+d=t-t=0\), \(a+d=t-t=0\), etc., so indeed all equations hold. Thus one family is
\[
(t,-t,t,-t)\quad (t\in\mathbb R).
\]

Now we find any additional solutions where some factor vanishes by taking \(a=3\), \(b=5\), \(c=7\), \(d=9\) in combinations.

If \(a=3\), then the remaining equations are
\[
(b-5)(c+d)=0,\quad (c-7)(d+3)=0,\quad (d-9)(b+3)=0.
\]
Systematically checking possibilities:

- If \(c+d=0\), \(d=-c\). Then from \((c-7)(3+d)=0\) and \((d-9)(b+3)=0\):
  - \(c=7\Rightarrow d=-7\), and then \((-16)(b+3)=0\Rightarrow b=-3\). So \((3,-3,7,-7)\).
  - \(d=-3\Rightarrow c=3\), and then \((-12)(b+3)=0\Rightarrow b=-3\). So \((3,-3,3,-3)\).

- If \(b=5\), then
  \[
  (c-7)(d+3)=0,\quad (d-9)(8)=0,
  \]
  so \(d=9\), and then \((c-7)(12)=0\Rightarrow c=7\). So \((3,5,7,9)\).

These are all with \(a=3\).

By symmetry of the same cyclic structure, the only solutions obtained are
\[
(0,0,0,0),\quad (3,-3,3,-3),\quad (3,-3,7,-7),\quad (3,5,7,9).
\]

Now verify:
- \((0,0,0,0)\): obvious.
- \((3,-3,3,-3)\): each side is \(0\).
- \((3,-3,7,-7)\):
\[
ab+ac=3(-3)+3(7)=12=3(-3)+3(7),
\]
\[
bc+bd=(-3)(7)+(-3)(-7)=0=5(7-7),
\]
\[
ac+cd=3\cdot7+7(-7)= -28=7(3-7),
\]
\[
ad+bd=3(-7)+(-3)(-7)=0=9(3-3).
\]
- \((3,5,7,9)\): obvious since
\[
a=3,\ b=5,\ c=7,\ d=9.
\]

Hence all solutions are exactly these four quadruples.

Final answer: \((a,b,c,d)=(0,0,0,0),\ (3,-3,3,-3),\ (3,-3,7,-7),\ (3,5,7,9)\)
